comments:
Summary:
This paper presents a practical approach to automatic device placement for TensorFlow computation graphs using an attentional sequence-to-sequence model trained with policy gradients. The model proposes placements (mapping graph ops or co-location groups to devices), executes them to obtain running-time rewards (square-root of execution time), and uses REINFORCE with a moving-average baseline in an asynchronous distributed training setup. Experiments on Inception-V3, recurrent language models, and neural machine translation show non-trivial placements that outperform heuristic baselines (Single-GPU/CPU, Scotch, and expert placements) in both per-step and end-to-end training time.

Strengths:
- Addresses an important and practical systems-level ML problem: optimizing computation/communication tradeoffs for heterogeneous hardware.
- The approach is end-to-end: it optimizes directly for measured execution time rather than hand-crafted cost models.
- Use of seq2seq+attention to represent and predict placements is sensible and flexible; the distributed RL training design is pragmatic for noisy measurements.
- Empirical evaluation is reasonably broad: results on three canonical models (Inception-V3, RNNLM, NMT), with both single-step and full training-time comparisons, and clear speedups reported (up to ~3.5x single-step and ~20–28% end-to-end in some cases).
- The analysis (profiling device workloads and communication time) provides useful intuition for why learned placements help.

Weaknesses and concerns:
- Reliance on manual co-location heuristics: the approach requires pre-processing to create co-location groups (e.g., grouping LSTM cells, inception branches). The heuristics appear ad-hoc and their impact on final performance is not fully quantified. Automating or systematically studying this step would increase applicability and reproducibility.
- Hyperparameter and engineering details are partly underspecified in the provided text: values for the failing signal, policy network sizes, training hyperparameters (learning rates, K, controller/worker choices), and how long/expensive the RL search is (they give 12–27 hours but more detail would help) are important for reproduction. The text mentions some choices (e.g., initializing baseline with failing signal; ignoring first step when averaging) but full ablations are missing.
- Robustness/generalization: it is unclear whether learned placements generalize across batch sizes, model variants, or different machines. The method appears to re-run the RL search per graph/hardware setup, which may be costly and limit practicality in some settings.
- Comparisons are focused on Scotch and human baselines. Including other partitioners/placement tools (or more variants of Scotch/Metis styles) and more ablation baselines (e.g., random search with budget, or hill-climbing/local search) would strengthen claims that RL is the preferable search method.
- The method depends on noisy runtime measurements. While the authors mitigate this with repeated runs and moving baselines, stability and sensitivity to cluster noise could be discussed more thoroughly.

Suggestions for improvement:
- Provide ablation studies: effect of co-location grouping rules, attention vs. non-attentional decoder, impact of K (Monte Carlo samples), effect of baseline initialization and failing-signal choice.
- Report more reproducibility details: exact network architectures, embedding sizes, optimizer settings, failing signal values, and compute budget required for the RL search per model.
- Explore automatic grouping strategies or show how sensitive results are to different grouping heuristics.
- Study generalization: can placements learned for one batch size or machine transfer to others, reducing the need to re-run RL frequently?
- Expand baselines to include simple search heuristics (random-restart greedy, simulated annealing) and other graph partitioners when feasible.

Conclusion:
This is a strong and practically relevant paper that applies modern sequence prediction and RL tools to a real systems optimization problem. The approach is thoughtfully designed and empirically effective on representative deep models. The main limitations are in the reliance on manual grouping heuristics, some missing ablations and reproducibility details, and questions about generalization and search cost. After addressing those, the work would be highly valuable to the community. Overall I recommend acceptance (strongly positive).
